Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd put in maybe 18 hours a week, but it wasn't like I had a set schedule or anything. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to recorded lectures while doing other stuff or just zone out with my notes and talk through concepts to myself. Some weeks I'd cram everything in a few days, other weeks I'd spread it out more randomly. I wasn't super organized about it, but somehow I made it work.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I have extracurriculars that take up time, so I'd often have to shift my study sessions around those commitments. When deadlines were coming up, I'd buckle down more, but otherwise I'd just kind of drift through the week without a fixed schedule. It wasn't ideal, but I managed to keep my head above water most of the time.
Q3: Usually it was because I needed to check if something was due or to grab a file for an assignment. I wasn't one of those people who logged in daily just to stay on top of things. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a deadline and that would prompt me to go in. Other times a classmate would mention something in the discussion forum and I'd check it out, but that was pretty rare honestly.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my routine. It was more like a tool I used when I absolutely had to rather than something I engaged with regularly. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more often, but it never stuck as a habit. The interface just felt kind of tedious to navigate, so I avoided it when I could.
Q5: The video lectures were actually pretty useful for me since I learn better by listening. I'd watch them and sometimes re-listen to parts that were tricky. The assignment submission portal was obviously necessary, and I appreciated when instructions were clear there. Beyond that, I didn't really dig deep into other resources. If something explained a concept audibly or through discussion, I found it valuable.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums most of the time. I only posted twice the whole semester because it felt forced and I didn't get much out of reading other people's posts. The supplementary readings and extra materials got skipped a lot too. If it wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, I probably didn't bother with it. Time was limited and my anxiety made me prioritize only what felt essential.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to my grades or helped me understand something I was struggling with. If I could see how it would help me on an exam or assignment, I'd engage with it. Vague "enrichment" stuff didn't motivate me much. I needed to feel like there was a clear payoff, otherwise my brain just couldn't justify spending energy on it given everything else I had going on.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely different. Required stuff I'd do even if I was stressed or tired because I had no choice. Optional stuff? It basically didn't exist in my world. I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, but later never came. There's already so much pressure with the required work that adding optional tasks just felt overwhelming. My anxiety made it hard to take on anything that wasn't absolutely necessary.
Q9: Definitely. A lot of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then work through them on my own without being connected to the LMS. I also used my own notes extensively and would sometimes look up YouTube videos or other external explanations for concepts I didn't get. The LMS was just the starting point, not where the real learning happened for me.
Q10: There were tons of times like that. I'd print out materials or save them to my laptop and then study from those for hours. I'd also talk through concepts with friends from my extracurricular activities, which helped me process things auditorily. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. My best studying often happened when I was walking around campus listening to recorded lectures on my phone.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, and then that was it for my LMS interaction with that material. But I'd return to those files multiple times offline. Sometimes I'd re-watch a lecture three or four times on my own device before an exam. The LMS would only show one access, but I was actually engaging with it way more than the data suggested.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. My anxiety was spiking, I had a lot going on with my extracurriculars, and the workload felt crushing. There were days when I just couldn't bring myself to log in or engage with anything course-related. I'd feel paralyzed by everything I had to do. The mindfulness exercises they offered helped a bit, but it was still a struggle to push through those low points.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, the LMS was the first thing to go. I'd skip logging in and just work from whatever materials I already had downloaded. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars meant I had to triage constantly. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those modules because facing them felt too stressful. I'd circle back eventually, but usually at the last minute.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd open a video and let it play while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other stuff. Or I'd scroll through readings without actually absorbing anything. My body was technically engaging with the material, but mentally I was checked out. The anxiety made it hard to focus even when I was trying to study. Those sessions felt like wasted time afterward.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they felt pointless and anxiety-inducing. Writing something for others to judge? No thanks. I also avoided starting big assignments until close to the deadline because the scope felt overwhelming. I knew it was important, but my brain just couldn't handle it until the pressure became unavoidable. It's a terrible habit but the anxiety makes it hard to start things early.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. I'd barely touch the LMS until a deadline was approaching, then I'd suddenly become very active. My engagement was extremely concentrated around due dates. Without that external pressure, I'd drift and lose focus. It's not the healthiest pattern, but the deadline stress was often the only thing that could cut through my general anxiety and actually get me moving.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it motivated me to keep going. Seeing that my work was actually solid despite my scattered approach was reassuring. But if feedback was delayed or vague, it didn't really affect my behavior much. I needed that immediate reinforcement to feel like my efforts were paying off. The high assignment scores definitely helped my confidence even when I felt like I was barely holding it together.
Q18: The course structure was pretty standard, which was both good and bad. Clear deadlines helped me know what to prioritize. But the expectation to engage with discussions and optional materials didn't fit my style at all. I would have benefited from more flexibility or alternative ways to demonstrate engagement. The rigid structure sometimes felt suffocating when my anxiety was high.
Q19: Having video lectures was great for my auditory learning style, so that made engagement easier. But the discussion requirements felt forced and made me anxious, which actually pushed me away from the LMS more. The sheer volume of resources was overwhelming sometimes. I wished there was a clearer hierarchy of what was essential versus supplementary so I could focus my limited energy better.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for something they're not finding, or they're anxious and keep checking things without actually being able to focus on producing work. It could also be procrastination, like they're active in the system but avoiding the hard stuff. Sometimes high activity is just stress manifesting as busy work rather than productive engagement. I've definitely had moments like that myself.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. I didn't engage much with the LMS, but I still got a distinction because my actual learning happened elsewhere. I studied offline, talked through concepts with friends, and focused intensely when it mattered for assignments and exams. The LMS activity doesn't capture the real work I put in. Someone looking at my logs might think I was slacking, but I was just learning differently.
Q22: Course logs can show when you accessed things and how often, which gives some picture of engagement patterns. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of attention during online sessions, and learning that happens through conversations or external resources. They also can't measure understanding or effort. A student could have low log activity but be deeply engaged, or high activity while being totally checked out.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and putting in genuine effort to learn it, not just going through the motions. It's about mental presence, not just physical or digital presence. For me, engagement looks like talking through concepts until they click, even if that happens away from any trackable platform. It's internal more than external, which makes it hard to measure.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied from downloaded materials, the conversations I had with peers outside the platform, or the mental energy I spent wrestling with concepts on my own. It also wouldn't capture my anxiety and how much effort it took just to push through some days. My high exam scores came from real engagement that happened mostly offline. The data tells such a small part of my actual experience.
